Mercenary: Mercenary crews tend to reward decisive trades: identify the opposing scoring pieces early and force them to answer your threat range.
Protect fast scheme pieces while the main damage package pins the opponent's activation order.
Performer: Performer crews usually win through movement tricks, marker pressure, and forcing awkward target priority.
Prioritize models that can score while the leader controls where the fight happens.
Kin: Kin crews pressure lanes with guns and hand pressure, so resilient or mobile hires help avoid losing the game to early attrition.
Deny clean firing lanes and threaten their support pieces before the attrition engine stabilizes.
Obliteration: Obliteration crews use placement, bury, and tempo pressure; bring models that can either score independently or punish exposed arrivals.
Avoid overcommitting one flank unless your list can recover from displacement.

Viktoria Chambers, Ashes And Blood: This title wants a compact, aggressive plan with enough independent scoring to avoid becoming all-in on the twins.
The best support hires either extend threat range, protect the leaders, or score while the leaders trade.
Colette Du Bois, Smuggler: Colette values flexible action economy and marker interaction; pick pieces that exploit movement windows rather than only adding damage.
Look for hires that can turn a small positional edge into scoring pressure.
Croix, Overloaded: Croix, Overloaded asks whether you can survive ranged pressure while still contesting the strategy.
Models with mobility, concealment, control, or pressure on support pieces tend to matter more than raw durability alone.

interact: Prioritize models that can interact without costing the main damage package its activation pressure.
markers: Marker strategies reward crews that can place, protect, or remove markers while still threatening the opponent.
mobility: Mobility is most valuable when it converts into scoring position, not just threat extension.
enemyHalf: Enemy-half strategies favor pieces that can cross the centre line safely and still contribute after scoring.
spread: Spread scoring asks for independent models that can operate without constant master support.
center: Centre scoring rewards durable anchors and control pieces that can survive repeated contesting.
control: Control pieces matter when they deny scoring AP or force the opponent to spend high-value activations inefficiently.
durability: Durable models are strongest when they also contest scoring space or protect a carrier.
scheme: Scheme pressure should come from models that do not need the master to babysit their lane.
denial: Denial is most useful when it removes opposing scoring options without pulling your whole crew off plan.
killing: Killing strategies still need activation and position discipline; avoid taking only beaters with no scoring utility.
antiScheme: Anti-scheme tools should be fast enough to reach the marker or model they are meant to deny.
